definition: To scry is to see the hidden or distant by gazing into a reflective surface — a crystal, a mirror, a bowl of water. In Magic it is the keyword for looking at the top of your library and reordering it.
history: A filed-down *descry* ('to catch sight of'), from Old French *descrier*, 'to cry out'; the crystal-gazing sense dates to the 1500s and the world of John Dee's obsidian mirror. In 2004 Magic made *Scry* a keyword, giving a quiet divination word a precise new life — which is why it now lives almost entirely in rules text. The fuller story is in this term's Word History.

def_sources.label: Deborah Harkness, John Dee's Conversations with Angels
def_sources.abstract: Explores why the Elizabethan scholar John Dee spent his later years trying to talk to angels through a psychic medium, arguing these sessions grew naturally out of his earlier scientific and religious interests rather than being a sign he had lost his mind.

etymology.origin: Aphetic form of descry ← Old French descrier ‘to cry out, announce’
etymology.note: Scrying literally means ‘catching sight of’ — a shortened ‘descry.’ MTG turned this quiet divination word into one of its signature keywords.
etymology.hook: From ‘to spy out’ to a card mechanic.

essay.title: Scry: how a whisper-word became a keyword
essay.dek: A four-century-old crystal-gazing word acquired a brand-new technical meaning inside living memory.
essay.html:
Scry is a small miracle of English thrift — what's left when you file the front off descry, 'to catch sight of at a distance,' from Old French descrier, 'to cry out, announce.' The announcing faded and only the seeing remained; by the mid-1500s a scryer was a crystal-gazer, one who saw hidden or distant things in a reflective surface — the world of John Dee and his obsidian mirror.

For centuries it was a quiet, archaic word. Then Magic: The Gathering, in 2004, made Scry a keyword: look at the top N cards of your library, then reorder or bin them. A word that meant 'to see the hidden' became a mechanic for seeing — and gently rearranging — your own future. This study's Insights chart shows the result: 'scry' now lives overwhelmingly in rules text, a word that became a keyword inside living memory. Most words hide their history; scry wears a fresh coat of it.

sources.label: Deborah E. Harkness, John Dee's Conversations with Angels (on scrying practice)
sources.abstract: Explores why the Elizabethan scholar John Dee spent his later years trying to talk to angels through a psychic medium, arguing these sessions grew naturally out of his earlier scientific and religious interests rather than being a sign he had lost his mind.

flavor_snippets.html: Prized by wizards as a mobile <mark>scrying</mark> pool, it bears visions drawn from the depths of the sea.

mechanics_snippets.html:
When this creature enters, <mark>scry</mark> 1.
Exile this creature: Create a 1/1 white Human creature token.

Magecraft — Whenever you cast or copy an instant or sorcery spell, <mark>scry</mark> 1. If that spell's mana value is 5 or greater, create a 4/4 blue and red Elemental creature token. If that spell's mana value is 10 or greater, Zaffai deals 10 damage to an opponent chosen at random.

Flying
Whenever you cycle or discard another card, <mark>scry</mark> 1.
Cycling {U} ({U}, Discard this card: Draw a card.)
<mark>Scry</mark> 3, then you may reveal the top card of your library. If a creature card is revealed this way, draw a card.

{1}: This creature gets +1/-1 until end of turn.
When this creature dies, <mark>scry</mark> 2.
Whenever a creature you control dies, put a phyresis counter on Weatherlight Compleated. Then draw a card if it has seven or more phyresis counters on it. If it doesn't, <mark>scry</mark> 1.

{1}, Sacrifice this creature: Draw a card. Create a Sorcerer Role token attached to up to one other target creature you control. Activate only as a sorcery. (If you control another Role on it, put that one into the graveyard. Enchanted creature gets +1/+1 and has "Whenever this creature attacks, <mark>scry</mark> 1.")
